nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef; typically found near coral heads in clear oceanic water at depths of 1–50 m

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous; generally accepts high-quality frozen meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and finely chopped krill. Has been documented supplementing its diet with small live foods. In the wild, tends to feed on ectoparasites picked from host fish; in captivity it typically adapts well to prepared foods even without a cleaning role.

avoidWith: aggressive fish that may outcompete for food

summary: A tiny Caribbean cleaner goby generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. Tends to remove ectoparasites from other fish, is peaceful toward non-conspecifics, and adapts well to prepared foods in captivity.
careNotes: The Golden Neon Goby (Elacatinus evelynae) is also commonly sold as the Sharknose Goby or Gold Neon Goby. It is distinguished by a distinctive yellow-gold lateral stripe and functions as a cleaner fish in the wild, picking parasites from larger fish — behavior that may be observed in the home aquarium. It is generally peaceful toward other species but has been known to show territorial aggression toward conspecifics, particularly in smaller tanks. Sources suggest that groups of seven or more can coexist in larger, more spacious systems when introduced simultaneously. This species tends to stay near rockwork and coral heads, darting into crevices when startled. It has been reported to live approximately 12–24 months in captivity, though lifespan may vary considerably with husbandry quality. Note: the trade name 'Neon Gold Stripe Goby' is sometimes applied to Elacatinus figaro (Barber Goby), a related but distinct species endemic to Brazil — purchasers should confirm the scientific name when sourcing. KH of 8–12 is generally advised for stable reef alkalinity.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity with some regularity. Pairs form monogamous bonds and lay adhesive demersal eggs on hard substrates. Eggs have been reported to hatch in approximately 8 days; larvae are pelagic and generally require rotifers and zooplankton before graduating to larger foods. Captive breeding is considered achievable but larvae are delicate and require careful husbandry.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection page did not have a dedicated Golden Neon Goby listing; species data sourced from secondary and supplementary sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Primary data source for water parameters, size, temperament, and breeding notes. Confirmed adult size 4 cm, temperature 23–27°C, salinity 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name Elacatinus evelynae, taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), max size 4.0 cm, and distribution: Western Atlantic — Bahamas and Lesser Antilles to northern coast of South America.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (10–15 gal / ~38–57 L), temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025. Note: product title lists Elacatinus figaro — likely a data-entry error on that retailer's page; scientific name in URL and species description are consistent with E. evelynae husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn Blog
additional.notes: Additional cross-reference for care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), reef safety, and lifespan (~12–18 months). Corroborates 10-gal minimum for solitary individuals.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity; pairs lay demersal eggs that hatch in approximately 8 days. Larvae require zooplankton and rotifers. Achievable but larval rearing requires careful husbandry.
